coredata.dc:title: An innovative enzyme-free ratiometric determination of uric acid based on polyethyleneimine modified graphene quantum dots pretreated with periodate combined with eosin Y
coredata.dc:creator: Mahnashi, Mater H.
Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Shahin, Reem Y.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel enzyme-free ratiometric system was proposed for fluorescence sensing of uric acid (UA). A positively charged polyethyleneimine modified graphene quantum dots (PEI@GQDs) and negatively charged eosin Y were rationally admixed. The former acts as a fluorescence signal reporter while the latter represents an internal reference output. Addition of IO4 −, an oxidizing agent, quenched the fluorescence of PEI@GQDs due to the isomerization of surface CN groups, which can be chemically reduced by the addition of UA. Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission signal of eosin Y remained unchanged. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence ratio (F460/F543) of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system was linearly increased with increasing the concentration of UA in the range of 0.05–180 µM with a detection limit (S/N) of 0.006 µM. The presence of high concentrations of reducing biological compounds e.g. ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH)/cysteine (CYS) can affect the performance of the PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system. The pretreatment steps were sufficient to oxidize AA, while the addition of maleimide masked the interference from common GSH/CYS. The system imposes some advantages such as simplicity, reproducibility, reliability, selectivity, and sensitivity. The PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system was applied to detect UA in human serum and saliva samples. The results were compared with the results obtained from commercial UA kits, which well agreed, confirming the accuracy of the proposed system for the detection of UA. This strategy represents a new approach for the use of non-selective QDs as ideal probes for the detection of reducing substances with acceptable levels of selectivity and reliability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Uric acid
PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system
Eosin Y
Selectivity
Ratiometric response
Biological samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of the fluorescence “Turn-on” of PEI@GQDs using UA
Feasibility of UA detection and optimization of variables

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors would like to acknowledge the support of the Deputy for Research and Innovation-Ministry of Education, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia for this research through a grant ( NU/IFC/02/MRC/-/002 ) under the Institutional Funding Committee at Najran University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) PEI@GQDs TEM image (inset is particle distribution diagram); (B) XRD pattern of PEI@GQDs; (C) FT-IR spectra of (a) GQDs, (b) PEI, and (c) PEI@GQDs; (D) UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra.
(A) The influence of IO4 − concentration on the emission of PEI@GQDs. (B) Plot between F°/F ratio and concentration of IO4 − (µM).

(A) The influence of UA concentrations on the emission of the system (insets are color changes of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − in the presence of different UA concentrations). (B) Plot between F460/F543 ratio and concentration of UA (µM).
The selectivity of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system towards UA detection (55.0 µM) in the presence of 2.5 µM various interfering compounds.
Oxidation of PEI@GQDs by IO4 − and determination of UA.
Analytical parameters of the proposed and other reported systems for the determination of UA.
Analysis of human serum and saliva samples using PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system before and after UA addition (n = 5).

head.ce:title: An innovative enzyme-free ratiometric determination of uric acid based on polyethyleneimine modified graphene quantum dots pretreated with periodate combined with eosin Y

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Sphinx University, New Assiut City, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Sphinx University, New Assiut City, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel enzyme-free ratiometric system was proposed for fluorescence sensing of uric acid (UA). A positively charged polyethyleneimine modified graphene quantum dots (PEI@GQDs) and negatively charged eosin Y were rationally admixed. The former acts as a fluorescence signal reporter while the latter represents an internal reference output. Addition of IO4 −, an oxidizing agent, quenched the fluorescence of PEI@GQDs due to the isomerization of surface CN groups, which can be chemically reduced by the addition of UA. Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission signal of eosin Y remained unchanged. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence ratio (F460/F543) of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system was linearly increased with increasing the concentration of UA in the range of 0.05–180 µM with a detection limit (S/N) of 0.006 µM. The presence of high concentrations of reducing biological compounds e.g. ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH)/cysteine (CYS) can affect the performance of the PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system. The pretreatment steps were sufficient to oxidize AA, while the addition of maleimide masked the interference from common GSH/CYS. The system imposes some advantages such as simplicity, reproducibility, reliability, selectivity, and sensitivity. The PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system was applied to detect UA in human serum and saliva samples. The results were compared with the results obtained from commercial UA kits, which well agreed, confirming the accuracy of the proposed system for the detection of UA. This strategy represents a new approach for the use of non-selective QDs as ideal probes for the detection of reducing substances with acceptable levels of selectivity and reliability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA) is considered one of the closely related biomarker to the human health [1]. Elevated UA in the blood serum resulted in “hyperuricemia” that is often associated with many diseases such as cardiovascular diseases, renal dysfunction, stroke, and gout [2]. Thus, the construction of sensitive, reliable, selective, low-cost, and simple detection methods has a great importance in the early diagnosis of health-related disorders [3,4]. Optical methods e.g. fluorometry are simple, rapid, sensitive, easy-to-visualize, and convenient-to-operate, making them suitable for the detection of (bio) molecules [5–8]. The most popular techniques for the detection of UA are based on the uricase enzyme that catalyzes the decomposition of UA into H2O2 [9,10]. Although the selectivity offered by these methods, but it is costly and need special storage conditions for the enzyme. Moreover, many methods were based on the single-signal measurements, which suffer from external factors such as operational and instrumental conditions [11]. Therefore, a determination based on dual-signal readings is apparently more reliable.

Carbon-based nanomaterials dots are widely used for the establishment of fluorometric and colorimetric sensors [12–14]. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial, have attracted great attentions in the field of sensing as a result of high quantum yield, biocompatibility, and stability [15–17]. Moreover, the existence of plentiful OH and COOH groups facilitates the surface functionalization of GQDs, helping in the deployment of GQDs-based probes in which GQDs can act as electron donor or acceptor [18]. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), a hydrophilic polymeric structure, can be used as a precursor for the synthesis of GQDs and a surface passivating agent. Moreover, the stability, water-solubility, biocompatibility, and fluorescence quantum yield can be enhanced by addition of PEI [19].

Herein, an ultrasensitive and selective non-enzymatic ratiometric-based fluorescence sensing of UA was established. The sensing system consists of positively charged PEI-modified GQDs (PEI@GQDs) and negatively charged eosin, which are chemically combined via an electrostatic interaction. The NH2 groups on PEI@GQDs surface were oxidized after addition of periodate (IO4 −) ion into nitrone and hydroxyl amine, which favors the quenching of PEI@GQDs via isomerization of CN groups. Addition of UA, a strong reducing agent, recovered the fluorescence of PEI@GQDs as a result of preventing the formation of isomerized CN. Simultaneously, the yellowish-green fluorescence of eosin remained constant under the same experimental conditions. Therefore, the response ratio (I460/I543) was used to quantitatively estimate UA accurately. Moreover, the PEI@GQDs/ IO4 − system was successfully applied to detect UA in human blood serum and saliva samples.

UA (>97%), graphite powder (GR), cysteine (CYS, >98%), dopamine (DA, >98%), ascorbic acid (AA, >97%), histamine (HIS, >98%), tyramine (TYR, >97%), lysine (LYS, >97%), glutathione (GSH, >98%), polyethyleneimine (PEI, >98%), and Tris HCl buffer were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, Germany. Glucose (GLU, >98%), methionine (MET, >98%), potassium periodate (KIO4, AR), and eosin Y (AR) were purchased from Merck, Germany.

Spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu RF-5301 PC) with slit width of 3 nm was used to collect excitation and emission spectra after each measurement. The morphology of PEI@GQDs was monitored using TEM (JEOL, JEM-100CX II-unit, USA). FT-IR measurements were carried out on Nicolet TM iSTM10 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCA Ulvac-PHI 1600, USA) was used to reveal the functional groups of PEI@GQDs. The X-ray diffraction spectrometer (XRD, Philips-FEI, Netherlands) PW 1710 was used to investigate the peak diffraction. EDX was evaluated using NEX QC + QuantEZ (Oxford, USA).

The synthesis of PEI@GQDs was conducted using simple hydrothermal method as follows: 0.5 g GR powder was mixed with 1.5 mL PEI and 25 mL deionized water (DI), and the mixture was ultrasonicated for 15 min. The suspension was autoclaved at 170 °C for 4 h. Then, the brown-black product was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 15 min before filtration using medium sized filter paper. The obtained filtrate was dialyzed using dialysis bag (3 KDa) before freeze-drying to obtain the final solid product.
See Electronic Supplementary Materials.

110 µL of 10.0 mg mL−1 of PEI@GQDs and 140 µL of 15.0 µM KIO4 were mixed at room temperature (RT) for 2 min, followed by 100 µL of 0.05 mM eosin Y. Finally, different volumes of 30 µM UA were added before completing the volume to 1.0 mL with Tris HCl buffer (pH 8.0). The resultant solution was incubated at RT for 5 min before measurement at 460 nm after excitation at 345 nm.

Anonymized human serum were obtained from Assiut University hospitals, Assiut, Egypt, and stored at 4 °C until analysis. For preparation of saliva samples, 0.5 mL of saliva was collected using saliva disposable tubes, and centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 20 min before storing at 20 °C until analysis. The biological samples were measured using the proposed fluorometric method before and after spiking with different concentrations of UA [20–23].

Different techniques were used to characterize PEI@GQDs including TEM, XRD, FT-IR, and XPS. Fig. 1 A explores the TEM image of PEI@GQDs with an average diameter of 3.8 ± 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). The XRD pattern of PEI@GQDs was shown in Fig. 1B with diffraction peaks at 26.88° and 42.79°, assigning to the (002) and (101) diffraction angels of carbons, respectively [24]. Fig. 1C (a) explores the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs with sharp bands at 3455, 1705, and 1620 cm−1, which are assigned to υ (OH), υ (CO), and δ (OH), respectively [25,26]. The FT-IR spectrum of PEI was shown in Fig. 1C (b) with distinctive bands at 3390, 1615, and 1325 cm−1, which are assigned to υ (NH), δ (NH), and υ (C-N), respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of the interactive product of PEI and GQDs was explored in Fig. 1C (c). The UV/Vis and fluorescence spectra of PEI@GQDs were investigated in Fig. 1D where the sensor explores absorption bands at 285 nm and 330 nm, assigning the existence of π-π* and n-π*, respectively. In addition, the PEI@GQDs hybrid shows an emission band at 460 nm after excitation at 345 nm. In addition, XPS was used to reveal the surface functional groups of the as-prepared PEI@GQDs (See supporting information, Fig. S1). The influence of different excitation wavelengths on the emission behaviors of PEI@GQDs were investigated in Fig. S2. It was found that the optimum emission was attained after excitation of PEI@GQDs at 345 nm. The stability of PEI@GQDs was investigated under various conditions including the influence of temperature, ionic strengths, pH, and irradiation times. It was found that the fluorescence readings of PEI@GQDs are stable under different conditions; except for increasing the temperature, the fluorescence readings were decreased (Fig. S3). The EDX spectrogram of PEI@GQDs was provided in Fig. S4 with corresponding peaks of C, O, and N. The above-mentioned results confirmed the successful preparation of fluorescent PEI@GQDs. Moreover, the quantum yield was calculated and found to be 37.8% against quinine sulfate as a standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the fluorescence “Turn-on” of PEI@GQDs using UA
ce:section.ce:para: Addition of IO4 −, a strong oxidizing agent, converts amino (NH2) group on the surface of PEI@GQDs into nitrone (CN+ − O−) and hydroxylamine (C–N–OH) [27], leading to the formation of less emissive PEI@GQDs at ambient temperature as a result of isomerization of CN moieties [28,29]. Addition of UA, a strong reducing agent, the formation of nitrone on the surface of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − was reduced, which hindered the isomerization of CN [30], enhancing the fluorescence emission of PEI@GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of UA detection and optimization of variables
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of UA is based on a rational blending of PEI@GQDs and eosin Y, which is achieved via electrostatic interaction. Fig. S5a exhibits the fluorescence of eosin Y at 543 nm after excitation at 522 nm. In addition, the emission spectra of PEI@GQDs, eosin Y, and PEI@GQDs/eosin Y were observed at 460 nm, 543 nm, and 460/543 nm, respectively (Fig. S5b). The Zeta potentials of eosin Y, PEI@GQDs, and PEI@GQDs/eosin Y were measured as −12.76 mV, +4.34 mV, and −5.33 mV, respectively, suggesting the interaction between PEI@GQDs and eosin Y is an electrostatic interaction (Fig. S5c). It was found that IO4 − oxidized PEI@GQDs leading to the quenching of PEI@GQDs fluorescence emission, while the fluorescence of eosin Y was not altered by IO4 − existence. Addition of UA resulted in recovering the fluorescence emission as a result of reducing the nitrone bearing PEI@GQDs (Fig. S5d).

In order to obtain the highest sensitivity of the system, factors affecting the determination were optimized including ion type, concentration of IO4 −, quenching time, fluorescence recovery time, and pH of solution. Fig. S6 explores the influence of different ions on the quenching of PEI@GQDs including IO4 −, Al3+, Ag+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Bi3+, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, F−, S2−, BrO3 −, ClO3 −, NO3 −, and NO2 −. It was concluded that IO4 − quenched the fluorescence emission of PEI@GQDs efficiently among these ions, suggesting the selectivity of PEI@GQDs towards IO4 −. Fig. 2 shows the influence of different IO4 − concentration on the quenching of PEI@GQDs in the range of 0.05–15.0 µM (R2 = 0.9988, F°/F = 1.04 + 0.31CIO4−). Therefore, 15.0 µM IO4 − was used in subsequent studies as the highest concentration to produce the lowest fluorescence of PEI@GQDs. Moreover, the influence of the incubation time on the fluorescence quenching of PEI@GQDs pretreated with 15.0 µM IO4 − was investigated in Fig. S7. It is seen that maximum quenching of PEI@GQDs was obtained after 2 min. The effect of incubation time on the recovery of PEI@GQDs pretreated with 15.0 µM IO4 − after the addition of UA was investigated in the range of 1.0-8.0 min (Fig. S8). It is observed that the optimum ratio of F543/F460 was obtained after 5.0 min. Thus, 5.0 min was considered as an ideal incubation for the recovery of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − by UA. In addition, the effect of solution pH on the interaction of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − and UA was evaluated. It was found the maximum ratio of F543/F460 was obtained at pH 8.0 (Fig. S9). Thus, pH 8.0 was selected as an ideal value for the interaction between the system and UA.

Fig. 3 A exhibits the fluorescence emissions of PEI@GQDs pretreated with IO4 − were gradually recovered after addition of UA while the fluorescence signal of eosin Y did not appreciably change. Thus, the ratio response (F460/F543) was linearly increased with successive addition of UA in the range of 0.05–180 µM with a linear regression of F460/F543 = 0.111 + 0.002CUA (R2 = 0.9989) and detection limit (LOD, S/N = 3) of 0.006 µM (Fig. 3B). The proposed sensor was compared with other reported sensors for determination of UA. It was found that the system exhibits wide-linear range and lowest detection limit, suggesting its application for UA detection in real samples with good reliability (Table 1 ). In addition, a calibration plot without the addition of eosin Y was constructed (Fig. S10). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of PEI@GQDs pretreated with IO4 − was increased linearly with increasing the concentration of UA in the range of 0.05–180 µM (R2 = 0.9943) with a detection limit (LOD, S/N = 3) of 0.006 µM, suggesting that the role of eosin Y is only the increase of the method reliability.

For studying the selectivity of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system towards UA detection, 2.5 µM interfering compounds were tested including cysteine (CYS), ascorbic acid (AA), histamine (HIS), tyramine (TYR), lysine (LYS), glutathione (GSH), glucose (GLU), methionine (METH), and dopamine (DA). Fig. 4 exhibited that UA produced a significant enhancement of the PEI@GQDs fluorescence compared to the other interfering compounds. It is noteworthy mentioning that high concentrations of reducing compounds e.g. CYS, AA, GSH, and DA can affect the results of the analysis. Thus, the elimination of interference was important to obtain reliable results for the determination of UA. Steps for pretreatment of samples were sufficient to oxidize AA. The concentrations of DA in human saliva and serum are 0.5 nM and 195.8 pM, respectively [4,35], which means the selectivity of the proposed system for the determination of UA in the presence of these concentrations. The interference from GSH and CYS can be inhibited by addition of maleimide [11].

The reproducibility of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system was evaluated via fabrication of eight different systems prepared under the same conditions, and their fluorescence responses were recorded in the presence of 40.0 µM UA (Fig. S11A). It was found that the RSD% value did not exceed 3.8%, confirming the reproducibility of the system for UA measurement. Moreover, the stability was evaluated by measuring the relative activity% (RA%) for five weeks where it was found that the proposed system retained the same% RA during this period, suggesting the stability of the of PEI@GQDs/IO4 − system for measuring UA (Fig. S11B).

In this research paper, a rational combination between positively charged PEI@GQDs and negatively charged eosin Y via electrostatic interaction was proposed for reliable determination of UA. Herein, the fluorescent PEI@GQDs and eosin Y were used as a signal reporter and internal reference signal, respectively. Addition of IO4 −, a strong oxidizing agent, quenched the fluorescence signal of PEI@GQDs as a result of the formation of isomerized CN on the surface of PEI@GQDs. Addition of UA, a strong reducing agent, restored the fluorescence emission of PEI@GQDs pretreated with IO4 − as a result of inhibition the formation of isomerized CN groups. Simultaneously, the emission signal of eosin Y remained constant. The main advantages of PEI@GQDs/ IO4 − are wide-linear dynamic range, low detection limit, good selectivity, and reasonable stability and reproducibility. The interference from high concentrations of common biological interfering agents with reducing ability was prevented by pretreatment steps and addition of maleimide. The proposed system was applied to detect UA in human serum and saliva samples with acceptable results.

Mater H. Mahnashi: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Reem Y. Shahin: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to acknowledge the support of theDeputy for Research and Innovation-Ministry of Education, Kingdom of Saudi Arabiafor this research through a grant (NU/IFC/02/MRC/-/002) under theInstitutional Funding Committee at Najran University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109062.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Double-signal quantification of amoxicillin based on interaction with 4-aminoantipyrine at copper and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots as an artificial nanozyme
Microchem. Acta
